topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile efficiency of a system notification. Subject line: Transition Notice. My stomach tightened instantly, a cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers hovering over the screen, rereading the sparse text. There was no farewell, no note of thanks, just a confirmation of departure. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and stared at the blank screen as if expecting the words to materialize. A slow, unsteady breath escaped my lungs. For a full minute, I didn't move, just absorbing the finality of the message, the sudden vacuum where years of guidance used to be. When I finally pushed myself up, the movement felt clumsy, my gaze drifting towards the empty corner of the office where his desk used to be.

[story 2]

I found him in the hallway, ostensibly to ask a quick question about the quarterly reports. He was standing by the water cooler, looking remarkably composed, his usual energetic stride replaced by a measured, almost slow pace. When I approached, the air seemed to thicken around him. "Mr. Harrison?" I managed, my voice sounding thin, reedy. He turned, and the pleasant, familiar warmth in his eyes was replaced by a distant, almost guarded look. "Yes, Alex," he replied, his tone perfectly level, devoid of the usual easy banter. He offered a slight, almost imperceptible nod, then continued walking towards the exit without offering a hand or a word. I stood there, rooted to the spot, my mouth slightly agape, unable to process the sudden absence of his usual presence.

[story 3]

The official announcement was posted on the departmental bulletin board, a crisp piece of paper tacked onto the corkboard. My eyes tracked the words, but they seemed to swim, refusing to settle on the details. He was gone. Retired. The reality of it settled over me like a heavy blanket, suffocating the oxygen in the room. I picked up the notice again, tracing the lines of his signature, a familiar script now feeling alien and distant. A strange, hollow feeling bloomed in my chest, a sensation like a sudden drop in temperature. I blinked rapidly, trying to clear the blurring vision, but the image of him, standing there, waiting for a conversation that wouldn't happen, remained stubbornly fixed in my mind.
